original_abstract: Active site architecture and metal–metal interactions in bimetallic iron–metal–nitrogen–carbon (FeMNC) catalysts remain critical yet poorly understood factors in enhancing the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acidic media. In this study, we perform a comparative structural and electrochemical analysis of FeSnNC and FeCoNC catalysts against their monometallic FeNC and SnNC counterparts. Deconvolution of catalytic activity using CO cryo-chemisorption reveals that while the bimetallic systems possess a site density of M-Nx centers approximately half that of the parent materials, they exhibit 50–100% higher mass activity compared to FeNC. This enhancement is driven by a significant increase in turnover frequency (TOF). Multi-scale characterization via electron microscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy confirms the coexistence of independent Fe-Nx and Sn-Nx or Co-Nx moieties, with no evidence of binuclear Fe–M–Nx configurations. Furthermore, 57Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy indicates that the introduction of a secondary metal shifts the active site distribution in favor of the D1 spectral component (associated with a higher TOF) over the D2 component. Our findings demonstrate that secondary metal doping serves as a structural modifier that optimizes the intrinsic reactivity of iron-based sites, offering a strategic pathway for designing high-performance PGM-free electrocatalysts.
humanized_abstract: The influence of active site structure and metal-metal interaction in bimetallic iron–metal–nitrogen–carbon (FeMNC) materials and their catalytic activity towards oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acidic solution remains poorly understood. This paper carries out the structural characterization and catalytic performances of FeSnNC and FeCoNC compared to their respective precursors FeNC and SnNC catalysts. CO cryo-chemisorption disclosed that the catalytic sites, M-Nx sites, in bimetallic systems were estimated to be about 50% in comparison to the monometallic FeNC and SnNC samples but exhibited 50–100% higher mass activity of the ORR. The increased TOF observed with the bimetallic materials suggested a higher active site activity for the ORR. Electron microscopy along with XAS evidenced the verification of coexistence of independent Fe-Nx and Sn-Nx and Co-Nx sites, without having any binuclear Fe–M–Nx sites. 57Fe Mössbauer spectra revealed an increase in the activity sites corresponding to D1 with secondary metal, compared to that corresponding to D2. Therefore, second metal acts as a structural modifier for enhancing the reactivity of iron centres in the FeNC catalyst material and hence act as a possible strategy for synthesis of PGM-free efficient catalyst material.

CO cryo-chemisorption disclosed that the catalytic sites, M-Nx sites, in bimetallic systems were estimated to be about 50% in comparison to the monometallic FeNC and SnNC samples but exhibited 50–100% higher mass activity of the ORR. The increased TOF observed with the bimetallic materials suggested a higher active site activity for the ORR. Electron microscopy along with XAS evidenced the verification of coexistence of independent Fe-Nx and Sn-Nx and Co-Nx sites, without having any binuclear Fe–M–Nx sites. 57Fe Mössbauer spectra revealed an increase in the activity sites corresponding to D1 with secondary metal, compared to that corresponding to D2. Therefore, second metal acts as a structural modifier for enhancing the reactivity of iron centres in the FeNC catalyst material and hence act as a possible strategy for synthesis of PGM-free efficient catalyst material.
